name: pipelines
context: List, inspect, trigger, retry, cancel, and read variables for GitLab pipelines with branch, status, and input-aware API calls
info: This skill covers pipeline operations in GitLab: list pipelines by project, ref, source, or status; inspect a single pipeline; trigger a new pipeline on a branch or tag; pass pipeline variables or typed inputs; retrieve the variables used by a pipeline; and retry or cancel an existing pipeline. Agents use it for CI/CD orchestration, deployment automation, test reruns, and release verification. The most important prerequisite is understanding how `.gitlab-ci.yml`, runners, and variables determine whether a pipeline exists and what jobs it contains.
triggers: list pipelines in a gitlab project
body:
# GitLab Pipelines

GitLab pipelines are project-scoped executions of `.gitlab-ci.yml`. The Pipelines API lets an agent inspect historical runs, create new branch or tag pipelines, retrieve pipeline variables, and retry or cancel existing runs.

```bash
python ./scripts/manage-pipelines.py create group/project main \
  --var DEPLOY_ENV=staging \
  --input level=3 \
  --input scan_security=false

python ./scripts/manage-pipelines.py retry group/project 311
```

The generic pipeline creation endpoint requires `ref` and optionally accepts `variables` plus a typed `inputs` object. Merge-request-specific pipelines are a separate API under merge requests, so if a job only appears for `merge_request_event` sources, load [[gitlab/merge-requests]] and [[gitlab/ci]] before assuming pipeline triggering is broken.

sections.title: List and inspect pipelines
sections.body: `GET /projects/:id/pipelines` filters by `ref`, `status`, `scope`, `source`, and date windows. Child pipelines are omitted unless `source=parent_pipeline` is requested. `GET /projects/:id/pipelines/:pipeline_id` returns the pipeline object with `status`, `source`, `web_url`, timing fields, and the `detailed_status` summary that agents usually need for reporting.

sections.title: Create pipelines with variables and inputs
sections.body: `POST /projects/:id/pipeline?ref=<branch>` creates a pipeline on a branch or tag. The request can include `variables` as an array of key-value hashes and `inputs` as a typed object. Inputs are the right place for structured runtime choices, while variables remain better for secrets and environment values stored outside the repository.

sections.title: Retry and cancel behavior
sections.body: `POST /projects/:id/pipelines/:pipeline_id/retry` retries failed or canceled jobs in a pipeline and is a clean way to recover from flaky infrastructure. `POST /projects/:id/pipelines/:pipeline_id/cancel` returns `200` even when the pipeline is already complete, so cancellation automation should inspect the returned status rather than assuming the call changed state.

sections.title: Inspect pipeline variables
sections.body: `GET /projects/:id/pipelines/:pipeline_id/variables` returns the variable set attached to a pipeline. This is useful when debugging why a deploy ran against the wrong environment or proving which non-secret parameters were used. Sensitive project-level variables are usually managed separately through [[gitlab/variables]].

links.context: Pipelines can be controlled with PATs, OAuth, or CI job tokens
Job tokens are the safest credential for pipeline-to-pipeline automation inside CI
Understand `.gitlab-ci.yml`, runners, and rules before automating pipelines
Typed pipeline inputs change how trigger requests should be constructed
Every pipeline belongs to a project path or ID
Merge-request-specific pipelines live under the MR workflow
Manage long-lived project variables outside pipeline trigger requests
Keep secrets in protected project variables instead of passing them as ad hoc trigger values
Comparable CI/CD orchestration workflow on GitHub

related.note: Pipelines API calls require a token that can access the target project
Pipeline behavior is defined by `.gitlab-ci.yml`, runner capacity, variables, and inputs
Typed inputs are one of the most important distinctions between different pipeline trigger flows
GitHub Actions is the closest equivalent CI/CD execution surface

scripts.name: Manage GitLab Pipelines
scripts.description: List, inspect, create, retry, cancel, and inspect variables for GitLab pipelines

GITLAB_TOKEN.description: Preferred API token; use unless OAUTH_TOKEN or CI_JOB_TOKEN is provided instead

CI_JOB_TOKEN.description: Optional CI job token for in-pipeline automation

GITLAB_HTTP_DEBUG.description: Set to 1 to print request details to stderr

ref.description: Branch or tag for pipeline creation

depends.reason: Authenticated access is required for private pipeline operations
Pipeline creation and debugging depends on understanding `.gitlab-ci.yml`, runners, variables, and inputs
